subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim of the investigation is clearly stated and its legal relevance in eyewitness reliability is fully explained
Misinformation effect and response-bias model are accurately described and explicitly linked to the study design
Both null and experimental hypotheses operationalise IV and DV with full quantification
weaknesses: Aim statement still reads as a fragment and could be rephrased into a complete sentence
Minor wording inconsistencies in the theoretical descriptions
The narrative link explaining how each model directly guided variable operationalisation could be slightly more concise
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the top-band descriptors: the aim and its relevance are not only stated but genuinely explained; the theory/model is described and the connection to this specific investigation is clear; and variables in the hypothesis are both named and operationalised. Although a few stylistic and minor linking issues remain, the overall presentation fully satisfies the band-6 standard, warranting the maximum 6 marks.

strengths: The independent-measures design is well explained with rationale on avoiding order effects
Sampling techniques (convenience and purposive) are described with clear justifications and demographic implications
Controlled variables (e.g., seating distance, timing) are described and procedural steps are detailed to enhance replicability
weaknesses: The procedural randomization method is referenced only in an appendix and not summarised in the text
Justification for controlling seating distance and choice of equipment is under-developed
Materials list functions more as an inventory without explicitly tying each item to experimental control
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands fully meet the top-band criteria with explanation (design and sampling) and the third (variables/materials) sits at the lower end of the top band. Overall this criterion clearly demonstrates explanatory depth and thus merits the full 4 marks.

strengths: Appropriate use of means, standard deviations and the MannâWhitney U test, with correct interpretation of U relative to critical values
Statistical findings are interpreted with reference to the data and clearly linked back to the hypothesis
The graph chosen directly addresses the hypothesis and uses error bars to represent variability
weaknesses: Axis labels and scaling on the bar chart lack full precision, reducing clarity
A minor data inconsistency appears between text-reported standard deviations and table figures
The description of the MannâWhitney U test could more accurately reflect its rank-sum basis rather than âmeansâ comparison
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite a few presentational and conceptual slips, the student applies both descriptive and inferential statistics appropriately and accurately, presents graphical findings that address the hypothesis, and interprets results in direct relation to the hypothesis. The strength and accuracy of the statistical reasoning justifies the award of the full 6 marks.

strengths: Findings are discussed with explicit reference to the misinformation-effect theory
Strengths and limitations are stated, explained and clearly tied to the studyâs design
Proposed modifications (broader sampling and culturally neutral materials) are explicitly linked to identified limitations
weaknesses: Discussion of response-bias theory and ecological validity remains underdeveloped
The depth of theoretical engagement in the findings discussion is limited
Only two modifications are offered, restricting the breadth of methodological improvement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand of evaluation demonstrates relevant description and explanation, fitting the mid-to-upper range of the 3â4 band. While there is room for deeper theoretical discussion and a broader set of improvements, the connections made between findings, theory, and limitations are sufficient to justify 4 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart with error bars is presented and clearly compares control vs experimental groups, addressing the hypothesis. However axis labels and scaling are somewhat unclear and numerical values on bars appear inconsistent with the text, indicating technical errors; thus mid-band (3).

The student states the statistical outcome, explicitly accepts the null hypothesis, and links this back to the predicted effect of leading questions. Interpretation is integrated with descriptive results, demonstrating clear linkage to the hypothesis. Minor brevity keeps the score at 5.

A null and an experimental (one-tailed) hypothesis are provided. IV (leading vs neutral questions) and DV (number of incorrect answers on critical questions) are not only named but quantified, showing full operationalisation. Slightly awkward phrasing in places keeps the score to 5 rather than 6.

Convenience and purposive techniques are named and a clear justification of each (cost, visual-acuity exclusion) is provided. Demographic details and implications for validity are explained, meeting the requirements of the higher band; 4 marks awarded.

Both the misinformation effect and response-bias model are accurately described, and seminal studies (Loftus & Palmer; McCloskey & Zaragoza) are summarised. The student explicitly states how elements of each study were adopted in the current investigation, demonstrating a clear explanatory link. Minor wording inaccuracies prevent the very top mark, so 5 is appropriate.

Findings are compared with the misinformation-effect theory, noting a contradiction. The discussion, however, is brief and lacks depth (e.g., no exploration of response-bias explanation). Therefore the strand sits in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

The aimââto investigate how leading questions with post-event information affect the number of incorrect answersââis clearly stated. The student explicitly explains why this is relevant, linking it to eyewitness reliability and legal reform. The explanation shows a solid, but not exceptional, depth; therefore the strand sits comfortably in the upper band but at the lower end (5 rather than 6).

Strengths (controlled setting) and limitations (convenience sampling, low generalisability) are stated and described with relevance to the investigation. Explanations are present but not fully elaborated or extensive, justifying 3 marks.

Several controlled variables (seating distance, timing, instructions) are described with some explanation of why they matter, and materials are listed. The reasoning behind each material choice is only partially developed, so the strand fits the lower end of the top band at 3.

A concrete modification (broader, more diverse sample and culturally neutral slides) is proposed and explicitly linked to the limitation of limited generalisability. Justification is adequate but lacks multiple, fully developed improvements; hence 3 marks in this band.

The independent-measures design is described and the rationale (avoiding fatigue/order effects) is explained. Controls such as standardised instructions and classroom set-up are discussed, evidencing an explanatory rather than descriptive level; hence top band for Exploration but full 4 marks.

Means, standard deviations and an appropriate Mann-Whitney U test are applied correctly for two independent non-parametric samples. Calculations are internally consistent and the conclusion matches the test outcome. Minor conceptual slips (describing MW as comparing means) prevent full 6, so 5 is assigned.

annotations.comment: The student provides clear definitions of the misinformation effect and response bias model, demonstrating solid understanding of theoretical frameworks relevant to memory distortion and its measurement.
The link between the theories and the current investigation is left incomplete, particularly where the discussion trails off; explicitly articulate how these models inform your study design.
Consider adding a concise statement explaining how the theoretical models guided the selection and operationalization of your independent and dependent variables.
Both null and experimental hypotheses are clearly stated, with the independent and dependent variables fully operationalized; this strengthens the studyâs predictive framework.
The aim statement reads as a fragment and lacks context; rephrase it into a complete sentence that conveys purpose and relevance explicitly.
Refine the description of your independent variable by specifying how âleading questions with post-event informationâ will be implemented and measured in practice.
The rationale for using an independent measures design is well explained, effectively addressing concerns about fatigue and order effects.
Random assignment references Appendix G but lacks a summary in the text; briefly outline the randomization procedure to enhance clarity.
While seating distance is controlled, the justification for this specific measure is not fully explained; clarify how this control reduces confounding variables.
Employing purposive sampling to exclude participants with visual impairments strengthens internal validity by ensuring performance reflects memory, not sensory limitations.
Include precise demographic details (e.g., gender distribution, cultural background percentages) alongside sampling techniques to contextualize your participant pool.
The equipment list reads as an inventory without linking each item to control strategies; explain how each piece of equipment supports experimental controls.
The detailed procedural list enhances replicability by specifying each step clearly, from random assignment to debriefing.
There is no mention of counterbalancing slide sets to mitigate order effects; consider varying slide order across participants.
Describe how you will monitor participantsâ adherence to instructions during the experiment (e.g., through direct observation or recording).
The rationale for selecting the mean and standard deviation is appropriate, aligning with the continuous nature of the data.
The descriptive table omits sample sizes for each group, which are essential for interpreting means and dispersion accurately.
A discrepancy is noted: the text cites a standard deviation of 25% for the experimental group, but the table lists 24%; verify and correct this inconsistency.
The bar chartâs axis labels and scaling lack precision, making it difficult to interpret group differences; refine labels and tick marks.
Enhance the graph by annotating error bars with exact values and including sample sizes to improve transparency.
Selecting the MannâWhitney U test appropriately addresses the non-normal distribution of your data and reflects sound statistical reasoning.
The interpretation of U=29 versus the critical value 23 is accurate, and the conclusion to accept the null hypothesis is well grounded.
Describing the MannâWhitney U test as comparing means misrepresents its basis in rank sums; revise this description to reflect its nonparametric nature.
The initial evaluation discussion ends abruptly and lacks depth; expand on how your findings relate to both the misinformation-effect and response-bias theories.
The discussion of ecological validity is acknowledged but remains superficial; deepen analysis of how real-world memory formation may differ from lab conditions.
The reflection on sampling bias and its impact on generalizability demonstrates critical evaluation of methodological constraints.
Consider linking your proposed modifications to specific ethical or methodological limitations (e.g., integrating suspicion checks to assess demand characteristics).
